questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-2,xmax=7,ymin=-4,ymax=8,xtick={-2,-1,...,7},ytick={-3,-1,...,7},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-1.8:6.8,samples=2] {x-2};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=-1.8:6.8,samples=2] {-x+6};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The graph shows $y=x-2$ and $y=-x+6$. What is their point of intersection?

options.why: Read the $y$-value before the $x$-value, reversing the coordinate order.
Read the $y$-intercept of $y=-x+6$ instead of where the two lines cross.
Read the $y$-intercept of $y=x-2$ instead of the shared point.

questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-2,xmax=5,ymin=-3,ymax=9,xtick={-2,-1,...,5},ytick={-3,-1,...,9},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-0.4:3.2,samples=2] {3*x-1};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=-1.8:4.8,samples=2] {-x+7};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=2.5pt] coordinates {(2,5)};\node[above right,fill=white,inner sep=1pt] at (axis cs:2,5) {$(2,5)$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The graph shows $y=3x-1$ and $y=-x+7$ crossing at $(2,5)$. Which check confirms $(2,5)$ is the point of intersection?

options.why: Substituted the $y$-value $5$ for $x$ instead of using $x=2$.
Checked just one equation; the point must satisfy both lines.
Changed the $-1$ to $+1$ in the first equation when substituting.

questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-2,xmax=5,ymin=-5,ymax=9,xtick={-2,-1,...,5},ytick={-5,-3,...,9},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-1.8:4.8,samples=2] {x+2} node[pos=0.92,right,text=blue!70!black] {$A$};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=0.4:4.4,samples=2] {-2*x+8} node[pos=0.08,above,text=red!75!black] {$B$};\addplot[thick,green!50!black,dotted,<->,domain=0.1:3.6,samples=2] {3*x-4} node[pos=0.92,right,text=green!50!black] {$C$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Lines $A:y=x+2$, $B:y=-2x+8$ and $C:y=3x-4$ are shown. What is the point of intersection of lines $A$ and $B$?

options.why: Read where $A$ crosses $C$ instead of where $A$ crosses $B$.
Located the crossing of $A$ and $B$ but reversed its coordinates.
Read line $B$'s $y$-intercept instead of the crossing of $A$ and $B$.

questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-3,xmax=4,ymin=-6,ymax=8,xtick={-3,-2,...,4},ytick={-5,-3,...,7},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-2.8:3.2,samples=2] {2*x+1};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=-1.4:3.8,samples=2] {2*x-3};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The graph shows $y=2x+1$ and $y=2x-3$. What is their point of intersection?

options.text: There is none: the lines have the same gradient $2$, so they are parallel and never cross.
$(0,1)$, where the upper line meets the $y$-axis.
options.why: A $y$-intercept is where one line meets an axis, not where two lines cross; parallel lines share no point.

options.text: $(2,5)$, a point that lies on the upper line.
options.why: A point on one line only is not shared by both; these parallel lines never meet.
The midpoint of the two intercepts is not an intersection; parallel lines never actually meet.

questions.solution_text:
Both lines have gradient $2$.
Equal gradients means the lines are parallel.
There is no point of intersection: parallel lines never meet.

questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-4,xmax=3,ymin=-3,ymax=6,xtick={-4,-3,...,3},ytick={-3,-1,...,5},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-3.8:2.4,samples=2] {x+3};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=-3.8:2.4,samples=2] {-x+1};\addplot[only marks,mark=x,mark size=4pt] coordinates {(1,4)};\node[above right,fill=white,inner sep=1pt] at (axis cs:1,4) {$(1,4)$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
A learner marks $(1,4)$ as the intersection of $y=x+3$ and $y=-x+1$ because $(1,4)$ lies on $y=x+3$. What is the correct point of intersection?

options.why: Only checked $y=x+3$; the point fails $y=-x+1$ because $-1+1=0\ne4$.
Found the correct crossing but swapped its $x$- and $y$-coordinates.
Read the second line's $y$-intercept instead of where the lines cross.
